does: This survey systematically categorizes and reviews machine unlearning methods—techniques that remove a trained model's dependency on specific erased training data to support right-to-be-forgotten compliance. The paper classifies unlearning approaches into four primary scenarios: centralized unlearning (subdivided into exact and approximate methods), distributed and irregular data unlearning, unlearning verification, and privacy-security concerns. It provides detailed technical introduction to existing unlearning mechanisms, examining how different approaches achieve data removal while maintaining model utility. The survey also addresses federated unlearning for distributed settings and graph unlearning for structured data, establishing a comprehensive taxonomy of the field's current landscape and methodological diversity.
connects: This work builds on growing legal and ethical demands for privacy protection, particularly following right-to-be-forgotten legislation globally. It extends prior work on privacy-preserving machine learning, data deletion mechanisms, and differential privacy by focusing specifically on the unlearning problem where models must efficiently remove learned associations with particular training examples. The survey connects to earlier research on machine forgetting, membership inference attacks that motivated unlearning, and foundational work on certified privacy guarantees. It bridges techniques from federated learning, graph neural networks, and certified machine learning, synthesizing scattered literature on how to undo model training without full retraining—a problem that emerged as practical alternative to naive model reconstruction.
enables: This comprehensive survey establishes foundations for advancing unlearning research by identifying open problems and gaps in current methods. It points toward developing more efficient exact unlearning techniques that scale to large models, improving verification mechanisms to certify successful unlearning, and addressing unlearning in complex architectures like transformers. The work enables future research on unlearning's interaction with other privacy techniques, exploration of unlearning in distributed and decentralized systems, and development of formal frameworks quantifying unlearning guarantees. It supports regulatory compliance in machine learning services, enabling practical implementation of data deletion rights and building user trust. The taxonomy also clarifies connections between unlearning and related problems in continual learning, catastrophic forgetting, and model robustness.
